exemplar.title: M25 #5: To what extent do you agree with the claim "all models are wrong, but some are useful" (attributed to George Box)? Discuss with reference to mathematics and one other area of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: M25 #5: To what extent do you agree with the claim "all models are wrong, but some are useful" (attributed to George Box)? Discuss with reference to mathematics and one other area of knowledge.

strengths: Maintains a sustained, clear focus on the prescribed title by unpacking and returning to key terms throughout.
Effectively integrates two areas of knowledge with relevant examples (Russellâs Paradox and Mercator projection) to support a coherent central claim.
Offers clear, coherent arguments and acknowledges counter-considerations, demonstrating a structured line of reasoning.
weaknesses: Links to areas of knowledge are occasionally superficial, with limited scope in both mathematics and natural sciences.
Evaluation of alternative perspectives is brief and not weighed systematically against the main position.
Broader, meta-level implications are hinted at but remain under-developed, reducing the overall depth of analysis.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs essay aligns with the 5â6 band for Understanding Knowledge Questions: it remains firmly focused on the title, presents supported arguments and shows some awareness of different points of view. However, the treatment of AOK links is uneven, counter-arguments are not fully explored, and the implications of the discussion lack depth. These factors justify placing the work at the lower end of the band and assigning a score of 5, matching the official examinerâs holistic judgment.

annotations.comment: The opening paraphrase accurately restates the question but lacks critical insight and a clear knowledge-question framework. The student should introduce a focused knowledge question to guide the ensuing analysis and give the discussion stronger TOK direction.

Definitions of âwrongâ as merely incomplete and âusefulâ as any goal-achieving tool are simplistic. The student should explore contrasting interpretations and the deeper epistemic implications of these terms within modelling.

Claiming that mathematical models need no real-world testing oversimplifies mathematical practice. The student should acknowledge validation processes in mathematics (e.g., peer review, consistency checks) to strengthen the argument.
The Borges fictional map example is appropriate but needs clarification of how fictional models inform real epistemic practices and what this implies for knowledge creation in TOK.

The concept of a âvicious cycleâ of complexity is introduced without linking to established TOK vocabulary (e.g., paradigm shifts, refinement). Tie this to recognised TOK frameworks to enhance rigour.
The Bonini Paradox citation is listed but not engaged with analytically. The student should explain its epistemological significance and how it parallels or contrasts with Boxâs claim.

The meta-level reflection on the essay as an imperfect model is insightful. To strengthen this, the student could critically examine specific simplifications they have made and discuss resulting knowledge gaps.
The Einstein quote is pertinent but its analysis is superficial. The student should unpack how Einsteinâs principle refines or contrasts with Boxâs claim, giving concrete examples.
The link to TOK is vague. The student should explicitly state and interrogate a knowledge question and connect their discussion to core TOK concepts such as evidence, justification or perspective.
Reliance on a blog (Moritz, Uni Bremen) as a primary source reduces academic rigor. Recommend using peer-reviewed or authoritative philosophical discussions on modelling.

The example of axioms (1+1=2) is trivial and does not illustrate a modelâs âwrongness.â The student should analyze more substantial mathematical assumptions that reveal model limitations (e.g., non-Euclidean geometries).

Identification of Set Theory as a fundamental mathematical model is clear and appropriate. To deepen analysis, the student could compare it with other mathematical models such as statistical or geometric frameworks.

Presenting a simple set of even numbers fails to capture the complexity of set-theoretical modelling. A more nuanced example would better illustrate the abstractions and limitations inherent in this model.

The explanation of Russellâs Paradox is concise and effective. For TOK depth, link this paradox to broader issues such as coherence, refutability, and the role of counter-examples in knowledge production.
Using Russellâs original text is solid, but the student should also incorporate secondary analyses that discuss the broader impact on mathematical philosophy and epistemology.
Connecting set theoryâs flaws to modern computing effectively demonstrates a modelâs usefulness. However, the anecdotal reference to writing the essay distracts from a more scholarly tone.
The summary of ZermeloâFraenkel axioms is too brief. The student should elaborate on how these axioms address specific paradoxes and the epistemic shifts they introduced to mathematical modelling.

The Spherical Cow analogy vividly illustrates intentional oversimplification. To strengthen evaluation, discuss potential pitfalls where oversimplification leads to misleading or incomplete scientific conclusions.
The Nautilus article is engaging but represents popular science rather than peer-reviewed research. Recommend supplementing with scholarly literature to enhance credibility.

The McKinsey quote offers an interesting interpretation, but the student does not critique its applicability to different contexts. Analysing how varying scenarios affect the quoteâs relevance would deepen the evaluation.

Introduction of the Mercator projection in the context of natural sciences is apt. The student could further strengthen the argument by quantifying specific distortions and exploring their practical impact.
Citing an online âGeographic Pediaâ article for the Mercator projection lacks scholarly weight. Recommend referencing academic cartography textbooks or peer-reviewed geography journals.
Acknowledging the persistent inaccuracy of maps is apt. To deepen the evaluation, compare Mercator with alternative projections (e.g., GallâPeters) and discuss why some flawed models remain in use.

The reformulation âall models are wrong and therefore usefulâ is bold but requires counter-examples to test its robustness (e.g., models that lose utility when oversimplified). Incorporate a counter-perspective to evaluate this claim.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content:
The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
